title: FIRST LOOK into Google Antigravity CLI
summary: Google Antigravity is an agent-first software development platform that enables developers to build, orchestrate, and monitor AI agents. The announcement introduces Antigravity CLI, a lightweight terminal interface that connects AI agents directly to code editors, terminals, and browsers for autonomous development tasks.
writeup: This video introduces the Antigravity CLI, a terminal-native interface to the same agent and intelligence layer behind the Antigravity 2.0 desktop GUI. It walks through the core design choices: lower overhead than the GUI, full conversation export to the desktop with a single command, parallel sub-agents for reading codebases and generating code and tests simultaneously, and granular per-command user approval built directly into the shell. The through-line is that the terminal is treated as a first-class agentic environment rather than a fallback, with scripting hooks, custom slash commands, and in-flow background queries designed for developers who never want to leave their shell.
key_points: The Antigravity CLI runs the identical agent and intelligence as Antigravity 2.0, so there is no quality tradeoff for choosing the terminal over the desktop GUI, and a CLI session can be exported to the full GUI at any point with one command.

Sub-agents run in parallel inside a single CLI session: one agent reads the codebase architecture, another generates code across multiple files, and a third writes tests concurrently, compressing what formerly required many sequential turns into a few.

The /btw slash command lets the user ask a background question while a long build or test run is still active, avoiding the need to interrupt or wait; /fork branches the conversation instantly so an alternative approach can be explored without discarding the current state.

Every shell command and every file change the agent proposes is surfaced for explicit approval inside the terminal: Y executes, N rejects, giving the user full control over what touches the workspace.
The -p flag passes a prompt at startup, making the CLI scriptable and embeddable inside data pipelines, cron jobs, and automation scripts as a first-class use case rather than an afterthought.

Custom skills defined in the workspace automatically register as native slash commands with tab completion, allowing personal workflows and team playbooks to surface as first-class commands without any extra configuration.
